explicit_text: My name is Masayo Matsumoto, born in 1893 in Nagano Prefecture during the 26th year of Meiji. I was raised by my father, who was a Shinto priest and a public office employee, and my mother, who was very strict and religious. Despite many health challenges, I graduated from teacher's college in the Taishō period and later became a Christian, which caused tension in my family. I moved to the United States and have reflected often on my upbringing and faith.

explicit_text: I was born and raised in Nagano Prefecture, a place where my family had deep roots and connections. My father was involved in village politics there, and we lived near Mt. Later, I moved away but always remembered my home prefecture fondly.

explicit_text: I was born in 1893, which was the 26th year of the Meiji period, a time of great change in Japan. My father studied in Tokyo during this era, even though it was against his own father's wishes. The Meiji period shaped much of my family's history and values, including the influence of Shinto and the modernization of Japan that affected our lives.

explicit_text: My family was deeply connected to Shinto; my father was part of the Shinto priesthood for years, and our household observed many Shinto customs. My mother made us pray before the Shinto altar daily, and we were taught to respect the gods at shrines like Suwa Shrine. However, my father struggled with his belief in Shinto gods, seeing them as human creations, which caused some tension in our religious life.

explicit_text: My father went down to Tokyo in his twenties to study, despite his father's opposition. While there, he worked and studied simultaneously and met people like Hakubun Ito and a rickshaw driver from his village. Tokyo represented a place of opportunity and struggle for my father during the Meiji period, shaping our family story.

explicit_text: When I became very sick with rheumatism, pleurisy, and heart issues, my father sold property to send me to Nagoya University Hospital for six months of treatment.
